# Graphs analyzing pre-lecture desire from students.

## Analysis & Conclusion

Overall, there seems to be a strong reason for implementing pre-lecture videos. Among students who find the class difficult, there is a much higher desire for pre-lecture videos. This suggests that students who feel confused by the content believe these videos would be helpful. This pattern is especially apparent for students with 0–6 months of prior coding experience, where the positive correlation is much stronger than for all students. Additionally, the overwhelming majority of students reported at least a 5 for their desire for pre-lecture videos. This includes students who do not find the class overly difficult, as they would likely still want a refresher to better support their learning.

While class difficulty and desire for pre-lecture videos appear to show a clear relationship, class understanding does not seem to be correlated with pre-lecture videos. There appears to be a slight negative correlation between understanding and desire for these videos, but overall the results remain fairly consistent. However, average understanding levels stayed between 4–5 across all levels of desire for pre-lecture videos.

The potential costs for this would mainly be the time associated for teachers and/or TAs. This could be done by recording class lectures for teacher and posting them the following year. One downside to this would be that students who were confused by the information before wouldn't hear it described in a new way. If TAs created the videos, there would be more time needed to create these videos. However, a potential benefit would be that the explanation from the TA and teacher would be different, so students may understand the content better.

One potential extension of this idea would be to look at other categories with low_exp. There seemed to be a major change in the correlation when our data was all students vs only looking at students with a low prior coding experience. Additionally, we could look at how pre-lecture videos would change by year. There could be a lot more intrigue from freshmen and sophomores who are more willing to watch pre-lecture videos than seniors who don't care about their grade as much.
